planting.note: Plant dormant cuttings or container-grown vines in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Higgins is a female-flowered (pistillate) variety that requires a perfect-flowered pollinator vine nearby. Plant within 50 feet of Ison, Triumph, or another perfect-flowered variety. In zone 9 Higgins ripens in August–September. The very large fruit size makes it one of the most impressive muscadines at the market and fresh-eating table.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil and provide a pollinator vine; Higgins is a Georgia Experiment Station-released muscadine variety renowned for producing fruits of extraordinary size — large white (bronze-skinned) muscadines reaching 1.5–2 inches in diameter, among the very largest in the muscadine world. The large, pale bronze-gold fruits have a sweet, rich, floral muscadine flavor and a tender skin that makes fresh eating considerably more pleasant than older tough-skinned varieties. Higgins is primarily a fresh-eating variety; the impressive fruit size creates visual impact at farmers' markets and U-pick operations. As a female-flowered (pistillate) variety, Higgins requires a perfect-flowered pollinator nearby — without cross-pollination the vine sets little or no fruit. Plant alongside Ison, Triumph, or another self-fertile muscadine for reliable production. In zone 9 the long, warm summer provides ideal conditions for the large fruits to develop to their full potential size.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Higgins Muscadine Cultivar
North Carolina State University Extension — Large-Fruited Muscadine Varieties
Mississippi State University Extension — Muscadine Fresh Market Production

planting.note: Plant dormant cuttings or container-grown vines in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Supreme is a female-flowered (pistillate) variety requiring a perfect-flowered pollinator vine within 50 feet. Plant with Ison, Triumph, or another self-fertile variety. In zone 9 Supreme ripens September–October, among the latest-ripening muscadines, extending the fresh muscadine season well into fall.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with a perfect-flowered pollinator vine; Supreme is a USDA-released dark (black-skinned) muscadine producing the largest black-skinned fruits of any commercially available muscadine variety — round to slightly oval berries reaching 1.5–2 inches in diameter with deep blue-black to jet-black skin and rich, deeply flavored, sweet-tart purple flesh. The combination of very large fruit size, deep color, and late ripening (September–October in zone 9) makes Supreme particularly valuable for extending the fresh muscadine season after most other varieties have finished. The deep color indicates high anthocyanin content and the associated antioxidant capacity that has made muscadine grapes a focus of nutritional research. Supreme is primarily a fresh-eating variety; the very large individual berry size and attractive appearance make it outstanding for fresh market and U-pick use. As a pistillate variety, it requires a self-fertile pollinator nearby for any fruit production.
sources: USDA ARS Small Fruits Research Station Byron GA — Supreme Muscadine Release
University of Georgia Extension — Late-Season Black Muscadine Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Large-Fruited Black Muscadines

planting.note: Plant dormant cuttings or container-grown vines in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Darlene is a perfect-flowered (self-fertile) muscadine that sets crops reliably without a pollinator. In zone 9 it ripens in August–September. The balanced, sweet fruit and self-fertile habit make it an excellent single-vine option for small gardens.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Darlene is a perfect-flowered (self-fertile) bronze muscadine released by the University of Georgia for home garden and commercial production, valued for combining good fresh-eating fruit quality with the self-fertility that allows a single vine to produce a full crop. The medium to large, bronze-gold fruits have a sweet, mild, pleasant muscadine flavor with less of the strong musky note that some consumers find overpowering in older varieties. The tender skin and mild, sweet flavor make Darlene one of the more approachable fresh-eating muscadines for people new to the variety. The self-fertile habit is particularly valuable for home gardeners who have space for only one vine; unlike female-flowered varieties that require a separate pollinator, Darlene sets fruit reliably on its own. In zone 9 the vigorous vine grows readily in the heat and humidity of Gulf Coast summers and produces consistently.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Darlene Muscadine Cultivar
North Carolina State University Extension — Self-Fertile Muscadine Varieties
Mississippi State University Extension — Muscadine Home Garden Selection

planting.note: Plant dormant canes or container plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Prime-Jan is a primocane-fruiting variety — it produces fruit on first-year canes (primocanes) in addition to second-year floricanes. In zone 9 the primocane crop ripens August–September and the floricane crop in May–June, providing two distinct harvest periods. Erect habit requires no trellis.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Prime-Jan is a University of Arkansas thornless erect blackberry notable for its primocane-fruiting (fall-bearing) character — unlike conventional blackberries that produce fruit only on second-year canes (floricanes), Prime-Jan produces fruit on first-year canes in late summer and fall (August–September) as well as a conventional floricane crop in spring (May–June). This dual-cropping habit provides two distinct harvest windows per season, approximately doubling the period of fresh blackberry production from a single planting. The primocane crop in late summer fills the gap between the spring blackberry season and fall, when no other thornless erect blackberries are producing. In zone 9 both crops ripen reliably, and the late-summer primocane crop avoids the rush of the spring berry season when many other fruits are also ripe. The fruit quality is comparable to other University of Arkansas erect varieties — medium to large, sweet-tart, jet-black berries with good flavor.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture — Prime-Jan Primocane Blackberry
University of Georgia Extension — Fall-Bearing Blackberry Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Primocane-Fruiting Blackberries

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant October–February. Prima Donna requires approximately 300–400 chill hours, suitable for zone 9a and the warmer parts of zone 9b. Cross-pollinate with another low-to-mid-chill southern highbush variety. In zone 9 it ripens in May–June.

directions: Plant in full sun in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), organically rich, well-drained, sandy soil; Prima Donna is a southern highbush blueberry selected by Fall Creek Farm & Nursery for its combination of high fruit quality and strong plant vigor in warm-climate conditions. The medium-large berries are firm, flavorful, and sweet with a pleasant balance of sugar and acid that makes them superior for fresh eating. Prima Donna's primary advantage is a high-quality, consistent fruit that stores and ships better than softer varieties — the firm texture retains quality for 2–3 weeks under refrigeration, significantly longer than some other southern varieties. In zone 9 home gardens this means less pressure to consume the harvest immediately after picking. Prima Donna is popular in commercial pick-your-own operations in the Southeast precisely because the firm berries withstand handling by customers without bruising. The plant habit is vigorous and upright, producing good yields without excessive vegetative growth.
sources: Fall Creek Farm & Nursery — Prima Donna Blueberry Notes
University of Georgia Extension — Commercial Southern Highbush Varieties
North Carolina State University Extension — Firm-Fruited Blueberry Varieties
